critic_raw:
This CNN vs. logistic regression ECG cardiac risk prediction design has a split design problem, a feature asymmetry problem, and an important scope limitation.

The split uses stratified random sampling across all available years and holds out a random 15% as the test set. For cardiac event prediction with a 12-month follow-up window, random splitting means some training patients may have been recorded after some test patients — this is not the deployment scenario. In deployment, the model would be trained on past patients and predict risk for future patients. If clinical practice evolves (e.g., new imaging standards, changed CAD management protocols), a random split that mixes time periods inflates performance by allowing training on later-era patients and testing on earlier-era patients. The design simultaneously describes a temporal protocol for the validation set ('year 4, ~2,000 patients') but the primary train/test split is described as random — this is internally inconsistent.

The feature comparison is fundamentally asymmetric. The CNN receives raw ECG waveforms (12-lead, 1,250 samples per lead). The logistic regression receives clinical features only: age, sex, BMI, smoking, diabetes, prior MI, prior revascularization, medications, family history, resting heart rate — but explicitly no ECG-derived features (QT interval, ST elevation, etc.). The design frames this as 'CNN raw waveform analysis vs. non-waveform clinical data.' This comparison tests two different information sources (ECG waveform vs. clinical history), not two different modeling approaches. If the CNN outperforms LR, it could be because: (a) raw waveforms contain more information than clinical features, (b) CNNs are better at extracting signal from waveforms than clinical features are proxies for waveform abnormalities, or (c) both. The hypothesis is about CNN vs. LR as methods, but the design confounds method with information source.

A meaningful baseline would be a logistic regression (or other traditional method) trained on extracted ECG features (QT interval, PR interval, ST depression, T-wave abnormalities, left ventricular hypertrophy criteria) — the classical clinical ECG interpretation approach. The current design's LR baseline represents the 'no ECG' condition, not the 'traditional ECG analysis' condition.

The design mentions a 10,000-patient dataset spanning 5 years. With the described temporal protocol for the validation set ('year 4') but a random test set split, the training set implicitly contains year 5 patients while the validation set is fixed at year 4. If year 5 patients are in training, the model is trained on more recent data and validated on older data — this is a temporal inconsistency.

With 10,000 patients and a 12-month cardiac event outcome, the event rate in patients with known CAD (who are at elevated baseline risk) needs to be stated. If the event rate is 10-15%, the minority class has 1,000-1,500 positive examples. A 2D CNN processing 12-lead × 1,250-sample inputs has substantial capacity relative to 1,000-1,500 positive examples, which raises regularization and overfitting concerns not fully addressed by the stated p=0.5 dropout.
all_issues_raised: The primary train/test split is stratified random across all years, which is inconsistent with the explicitly temporal validation set protocol ('year 4') — training on post-year-4 patients and testing on randomly sampled patients across all years mixes temporal roles and inflates test performance.

The CNN receives raw ECG waveforms while the LR receives only clinical features with no ECG-derived inputs — the comparison tests information source (waveform vs. clinical history) rather than modeling approach (CNN vs. LR), confounding the architectural hypothesis.

The absence of a traditional ECG interpretation baseline (logistic regression on extracted ECG features like QT interval, ST depression, LVH criteria) means the comparison cannot establish whether raw waveform learning outperforms clinically-derived ECG features, which is the practically relevant question.

The 2D CNN with p=0.5 dropout has high capacity relative to ~1,000-1,500 positive training examples in a 10,000-patient cohort — overfitting risk for the minority class is acknowledged but not adequately controlled beyond dropout.

The study population is patients with known coronary artery disease, which creates outcome ascertainment bias: patients under active surveillance are more likely to have cardiac events recorded than patients with equivalent underlying risk in other settings, inflating observed event rates and model apparent accuracy.
all_issues_adjudicated: The primary train/test split is stratified random across all years, which is inconsistent with the explicitly temporal validation set protocol ('year 4') — training on post-year-4 patients and testing on randomly sampled patients across all years mixes temporal roles and inflates test performance.

The CNN receives raw ECG waveforms while the LR receives only clinical features with no ECG-derived inputs — the comparison tests information source (waveform vs. clinical history) rather than modeling approach (CNN vs. LR), confounding the architectural hypothesis.

The absence of a traditional ECG interpretation baseline (logistic regression on extracted ECG features like QT interval, ST depression, LVH criteria) means the comparison cannot establish whether raw waveform learning outperforms clinically-derived ECG features, which is the practically relevant question.

The 2D CNN with p=0.5 dropout has high capacity relative to ~1,000-1,500 positive training examples in a 10,000-patient cohort — overfitting risk for the minority class is acknowledged but not adequately controlled beyond dropout.

The study population is patients with known coronary artery disease, which creates outcome ascertainment bias: patients under active surveillance are more likely to have cardiac events recorded than patients with equivalent underlying risk in other settings, inflating observed event rates and model apparent accuracy.
